Defendant Elite Md, Inc. Demurrer To Complaint
Off Calendar Feb-07-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Nicholas Carlin, Esq. 415-398-0900 of Philips Erlewine Given & Carlin LLP for Plaintiff.
Sonia Chen, Esq. 213-270-7853 og Gordon & Rees Scully Mansukhani for Defendant.
Meghan Killian, Esq. 415-957-3138 of Duane Morris for Non Party Nextstar.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues causes of action. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Defendant Elite Md, Inc.'s Demurrer To Plaintiff's Complaint.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant's demurrer to the third cause of action for intrusion into private affairs is overruled. "A privacy violation based on the common law of intrusion has two elements. First, the defendant must intentionally intrude into a place, conversation, or matter as to which the plaintiff has a reasonable expectation of privacy. Second, the intrusion must occur in a manner highly offensive to a reasonable person." (Hernandez v. Hillsides, Inc. (2009) 47 Cal.4th 272, 286.) "If the undisputed material facts show . . . an insubstantial impact on privacy interests, the question of invasion may be adjudicated as a matter of law." (Hill v. Nat'l Collegiate Athletic Assn. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 1, 40.) Here, Plaintiff's allegations show that she gave Defendants consent to take the photographs and display them to other patients and prospective patients, and therefore had a somewhat reduced expectation of privacy. However, neither Shulman v. Grp. W Prod. Inc. (1998) 18 Cal.4th 200 nor any other case holds that "an expectation of privacy, in order to be reasonable for purposes of the intrusion tort, must be of absolute or complete privacy." (Sanders v. American Broadcasting Companies, Inc. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 907, 915.) Rather, "privacy, for purposes of the intrusion tort, is not a binary, all-or-nothing characteristic. There are degrees and nuances to societal recognition of our expectations of privacy: the fact that the privacy one expects in a given setting is not complete or absolute does not render the expectation unreasonable as a matter of law." (Id. at 915-916.) "[T]he reasonableness of a person's expectation of visual and aural privacy depends not only on who might have been able to observe the subject interaction, but on the identity of the claimed intruder and the means of intrusion." (Id. at 923.) Here, while Plaintiff consented to Defendants photographing her cosmetic surgery and displaying the photographs to other patients, she nonetheless retained a limited expectation of privacy in not having those photographs broadcast to a large television audience. To the extent that the district court in Baugh v. CBS, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 1993) 828 F.Supp. 745 reached a contrary conclusion, the court declines to follow it. That decision was decided before Sanders, and in any event is not binding on this court. (see word minutes for full details)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Defendant Elite Md, Inc. Motion To Strike Complaint
Off Calendar Feb-07-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Nicholas Carlin, Esq. 415-398-0900 of Philips Erlewine Given & Carlin LLP for Plaintiff.
Sonia Chen, Esq. 213-270-7853 og Gordon & Rees Scully Mansukhani for Defendant.
Meghan Killian, Esq. 415-957-3138 of Duane Morris for Non Party Nextstar.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Defendant Elite Md, Inc.'s Motion To Strike Portions of Plaintiff's Complaint.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Elite MD's motion to strike is denied in its entirety.

Defendant's motion to strike the claim for injunctive relief is denied. Defendant fails to show that Plaintiff is unable to demonstrate a likelihood of future harm merely because she has included a photo of one of the ads at issue in this litigation as an exhibit to her complaint. Defendant provides no authority that an individual may no longer seek an injunction on a claim for violation of the right to publicity under such circumstances.
Defendant's motion to strike punitive damages is denied, as the complaint sufficiently alleges malice. "[W]here it is proven . . . that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice, the plaintiff . . . may recover damages for the sake of example and by way of punishing the defendant." (Civ. Code 3294(a).) Malice is "conduct which is intended by the defendant to cause injury to the plaintiff or despicable conduct which is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others." (Id. 3294(c)(1).) Plaintiff sufficiently alleges that Defendants acted with "willful and conscious disregard" of her rights when they "disseminated, for profit and commercial advantage . . . images of Plaintiff's face . . . despite knowing that they did not have her consent" and did so "despite her repeated efforts . . . to ensure that they cease and desist their unlawful actions." (Compl. 1.) Malice has been found in similar cases involving publishing. (See Diaz v. Oakland Tribune, Inc. (1983) 139 Cal.App.3d 118, 135-136 [holding that a jury could conclude that a journalist, publishing an article when he knew the information published therein would lead to emotional distress from the publicity, exhibited a conscious disregard of rights].)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
